original.query: In that duel format I described where turns are scored for how “beautiful” the magic looks and you can’t repeat a spell, there’s also a hidden effect that kicks in at the start of every turn and changes both competitors in opposite directions. If we go all the way to the final round, what should I expect to be the main reason my late-round spell choices and performance shift so dramatically compared with the opening round?

parsed_response.answer: In the final round of the duel format you described, the main reason your late-round spell choices and performance shift dramatically compared with the opening round is because the hidden effect that activates at the start of every turn changes both competitors in opposite directions. This means each new turn introduces a dynamic alteration influencing both players differently, which forces you to adapt your strategy and spell selection accordingly, making the late rounds distinctly different from the initial rounds.
parsed_response.reason: Memory m1 specifically talks about curiosity regarding spell mechanics and age regression consequences in roleplay scenarios, which relates to game mechanics and changing conditions during play. Although it’s brief, it best aligns with the described hidden effect and its impact on progressive rounds affecting spell choices and performance. This explains why the hidden effect causes shifts in gameplay and strategy as the duel advances to later rounds.

judge_output_raw.reason: The model's reason explicitly references memory m1, noting its content about curiosity regarding spell mechanics and age regression consequences in roleplay scenarios, and states it best aligns with the hidden effect and its impact on progressive rounds. This shows clear usage of the selected memory to support the explanation.
